topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the small, dimly lit bar felt suddenly thick, pressing down on Elias’s chest. He was nursing a lukewarm beer, trying to anchor himself to the familiar scent of stale smoke and spilled whiskey, when the opening chords hit. It was his melody, the one he’d poured three years of sleepless nights into—a fragile, yearning piece about lost light. But it wasn't coming from his worn acoustic guitar. It was coming from a stranger, someone with a voice that mirrored his own, yet somehow sharper, more polished. Elias froze, the beer sweating in his hand. His breath hitched, a tiny, involuntary sound escaping his throat. He watched the performer, his jaw tightening, every muscle in his neck suddenly rigid. He wanted to bolt, to disappear into the shadows, but his feet remained stubbornly planted on the sticky floor.

[story 2]

"That’s mine," she whispered, the sound cutting through the ambient noise like a shard of glass. Liam felt the blood drain from his face. He’d been waiting for his turn, a nervous energy humming beneath his skin, and then, suddenly, it was usurped. The song—his raw, deeply personal composition—was being spun by a guitarist in the corner, a figure bathed in the amber glow of a single spotlight. Liam’s hands started to tremble, the tremor spreading up his arms. He gripped the edge of the bar so hard his knuckles turned white, his eyes locked on the interloper. A strange, hot flush crept up his neck, making him acutely aware of the heat radiating off the crowd, the sudden, intense scrutiny of everyone around him. He couldn't move; he was trapped by the sheer audacity of the performance.

[story 3]

When the melody began, it was undeniably his. Every note, every subtle inflection, was a direct echo of the private world he’d built within his own head. But the execution was flawless, delivered with an effortless command that felt alien. Marcus leaned against the wall, his usual easy demeanor dissolving into something taut and brittle. He found himself unable to look away, his gaze fixed on the musician across the stage. A knot tightened low in his stomach, a sensation like ice water flooding his core. He started shifting his weight from one foot to the other, an unconscious attempt to physically disengage, but the sound pulled him forward, tethering him to the moment. He swallowed hard, tasting metallic fear, wishing desperately that the floor would simply swallow him whole.

When the melody began, it was undeniably his. Every note, every subtle inflection, was a direct echo of the private world he’d built within his own head. But the execution was flawless, delivered with an effortless command that felt alien. Marcus leaned against the wall, his usual easy demeanor dissolving into something taut and brittle.
